_pass: Level 3 Pass 1 + Rounds 1-3 visual polish + worked examples v2
title: Build a savings plan
deck: Three time horizons, three savings vehicles, three formulas from earlier in the chapter. Match the tool to the goal, and the math is already done.
objectives: Plan for short-, medium-, and long-term financial goals using lump-sum and annuity formulas inside savings vehicles.

content.text: Imagine three goals sitting on the same kitchen-table notepad. The first is a three-month emergency fund, the kind of cushion that prevents a flat tire from cascading into a missed rent payment. The second is a down payment on a house, a few years out. The third is a retirement account that will support us forty years from now. Three goals, three time horizons, three very different deposit patterns.

Money for next month and money for forty years from now cannot live in the same place. Cash that we may need on short notice belongs in something liquid and safe; cash with a long horizon belongs somewhere that compounds aggressively. This section maps the time horizon to the savings vehicle to the formula, ties together every tool we built earlier in the chapter, and shows that the most powerful lever on any savings outcome is, almost always, time.
3.6.1  Match the horizon to the vehicle

Three rough horizons cover most of a personal financial plan. Money needed within two years lives in a high-yield savings account (HYSA), which is liquid, FDIC-insured, and pays a modest APY. Money needed in three to seven years can step up to a certificate of deposit (CD), which trades a fixed term for a slightly higher APY. Money needed in ten or more years belongs in a retirement account (such as a 401(k) or IRA), which is invested in stocks and bonds and earns substantially more over long periods, at the cost of short-term volatility.

content.caption: Figure 3.6. Three time horizons, three savings vehicles, three target returns. Same chapter, three different formulas at work.

content.text: Definition 3.6. A savings plan is a future-value calculation done in advance. Given a target dollar amount, a time horizon, and an expected APY, we solve for the deposit (either lump-sum P or periodic PMT) that produces the target. The right formula depends on the deposit pattern: lump-sum problems use A = P(1 + r/n)nt recurring-deposit problems use the annuity formula A = PMT · [(1 + r/n)nt − 1] / (r/n).

Margin noteThe 4.5% / 5% / 7% rates in Figure 3.6 are mid-decade ballpark numbers for HYSA, CDs, and long-run stock returns; they will drift as the economy moves. The decision rule, “ match the vehicle to the horizon,” is durable even when the specific numbers are not.

When choosing a savings plan, the temptation is to focus on the rate. The far more powerful lever, in almost every case, is the number of years. Two graduates who save identical amounts at identical rates can end with wildly different balances if one starts ten years earlier than the other, because the formula's growth in t is exponential. The single most useful piece of personal-finance advice in this chapter is to start any long-horizon savings account as early as humanly possible.
The cheapest dollar in a retirement account is the one we deposited at 22, not the one we deposited at 52.

Example 3.6. Two siblings, two starting dates.

Two siblings each plan to retire at age 65, and each plan to contribute $300 per month to a retirement account earning 7% per year, compounded monthly. The older sibling starts at age 25 and contributes for 40 years. The younger sibling starts at age 35 and contributes for 30 years. We want both account balances at age 65, and the difference between them.

Solution.

Step 1. The older sibling: 40 years. Using the annuity formula with PMT = 300, r = 0.07, n = 12, t = 40:

A = 300 · [(1 + 0.07/12)480 − 1] / [0.07/12] ≈ $787,000

Step 2. The younger sibling: 30 years. Same formula with t = 30, so 360 periods:

A = 300 · [(1 + 0.07/12)360 − 1] / [0.07/12] ≈ $366,000

Step 3. Compare. The older sibling's balance is roughly $787,000 against the younger sibling's $366,000.

Step 4. Inspect the lifetime contributions. Older sibling: 300 × 12 × 40 = $144,000. Younger sibling: 300 × 12 × 30 = $108,000. The older sibling contributed only $36,000 more, but ended with about $421,000 more in the account. The interest, not the contributions, did all of the heavy lifting.

The extra decade was worth more than $400,000, on roughly $36,000 of additional deposits. That ratio is exactly what makes “ start as early as possible” the most repeated piece of advice in personal finance.

In the wildEvery retirement-readiness tool from Fidelity, Vanguard, Schwab, and your university's HR portal runs this same calculation under the hood. Employer 401(k) matches (the “ free money” everyone is told to take) are essentially boosters to the PMT argument in the annuity formula. The mathematics of this chapter is the only thing standing between “ saving sounds important” and “ here is a plan I can act on tomorrow.”

1. Sara wants $10,000 in an emergency fund three years from today. Her HYSA pays 4% APY, compounded monthly. What single lump sum, deposited today, reaches that goal? Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $8,870.93. Solving 10,000 = P(1 + 0.04/12)36 gives P &approx 10,000 / 1.12716 &approx 8,870.93.

  2. Marcus saves $200 a month into a 4% APY savings account, compounded monthly. What is the balance after 5 years? Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $13,259.74. A = 200 · [(1 + 0.04/12)60 − 1] / (0.04/12) &approx 13,259.74.

  3. A retirement account currently holds $20,000 and earns 7% per year, compounded monthly. How much will it be worth in 30 years if no further deposits are made? Round to the nearest cent.

    Show answer

    Answer. About $162,265. A = 20,000(1 + 0.07/12)360 &approx 162,265. Even with no further contributions, the compound-interest formula nearly does an 8× multiplier over thirty years.

  4. Which formula is the right one to use when the goal is a single deposit growing untouched for many years?

    1. Simple interest A = P(1 + rt)
    2. Lump-sum compound A = P(1 + r/n)nt
    3. Annuity A = PMT · [(1 + r/n)nt − 1] / (r/n)
    4. Continuous compounding A = Pert
    Show answer

    Answer. B (or D, for the same scenario with continuous compounding). The annuity formula is for repeated deposits; the simple-interest formula is for short-term arrangements without compounding.

  5. Which feature of a long-term savings plan has the largest effect on the final balance, all else equal?

    1. Doubling the monthly contribution
    2. Increasing the rate by 1 percentage point
    3. Saving for ten more years
    4. Switching between weekly and monthly compounding
    Show answer

    Answer. C. Time enters the formula as an exponent, so additional years produce the largest single boost. Compounding frequency, by Section 3.3, makes only a tiny difference once we are past monthly.

  6. Sara is choosing between an HYSA and a long-term CD for an emergency fund she may need within the next year. Which is the better fit, and why?

    1. The CD, because it pays a slightly higher APY
    2. The HYSA, because liquidity matters more than yield for short-horizon money
    3. Either; the math is identical
    4. A 30-year retirement account, to maximize compounding
    Show answer

    Answer. B. CDs lock up money for the term and charge an early-withdrawal penalty; HYSAs are fully liquid. For a fund that might be needed on short notice, the slightly lower APY is the right trade-off.

content.title: Reverse-engineer the monthly contribution
content.problem: You want to have **$50,000** saved in **5 years** for a down payment on a house. The account pays **5% APR compounded monthly**. How much do you need to save **each month**?
content.solution:
**Use the annuity formula and solve for M.**

A = M[(1 + r/n)^(nt) − 1] / (r/n), with A = 50,000, r = 0.05, n = 12, t = 5.

r/n = 0.0041666...; nt = 60.

(1.0041666)^60 ≈ 1.2834.

50,000 = M × (1.2834 − 1) / 0.0041666 = M × 0.2834 / 0.0041666 = M × 68.006

M = 50,000 / 68.006 ≈ **$735.31/month**

So $735/month for 60 months at 5% APR (monthly compounding) gets you to $50,000 — and about $5,880 of that is interest earned, not contributions.

content.title: The inflation gotcha
content.problem: Suppose you save **$300/month for 30 years** at **6% APR compounded monthly**, reaching **~$301,000** (as in Example 3.5.2). If average inflation is **3% per year**, what is that $301,000 worth in *today's* purchasing power?
content.solution:
**Discount the future value by 30 years of inflation.**

Inflation reduces purchasing power at a compound rate. The factor over 30 years at 3% is (1.03)^30 ≈ 2.427. So a future dollar in year 30 is worth about 1/2.427 ≈ **$0.41** in today's dollars.

$301,000 in year-30 dollars is worth roughly $301,000 / 2.427 ≈ **$124,000** in today's purchasing power.

*The takeaway.* That $301,000 nominal balance looks impressive, but real (inflation-adjusted) purchasing power is less than half. This is why long-horizon savings should target returns *above inflation* — 6% nominal − 3% inflation = 3% real, which is still meaningful but humbler than the headline.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Building a savings plan is the inverse problem of computing future value: pick the target, then solve for M. The annuity formula is the workhorse. Inflation should always be subtracted from nominal returns when judging real purchasing power decades into the future.

content.text: Six sections ago, “ interest”  was a single word that meant different things in different contexts. Now it is a small, well-organized toolkit: name the four pieces, pick the formula, plug the inputs in, interpret the answer. Every retirement plan, mortgage, student loan, credit-card statement, and Treasury auction in the real world is built on top of these formulas. Chapter 4 picks up where saving ends and borrowing begins, applying the same machinery, in reverse, to loans.
